sourceText: When Heaven creates all things, why must it bestow them only upon officials?
Emphasizes that ability and talent can exist regardless of status or position, showing Jeongjo's inclusive view on talent acquisition by broadening the scope for appointing capable individuals.
Without a heart that cares for the people, one cannot be called a monarch.
Highlights that the purpose of a monarch's existence is for the people, revealing a spirit of benevolent love for the populace, prioritizing them above all else.
Success is given to those who strive; cast aside vain desires and devote yourself to learning.
Emphasizes the importance of effort and learning, cautioning against vain desires and urging continuous self-improvement.
Frustration in pursuing an official career, turmoil during political upheaval, the gap between ideals and reality, and his later years of wandering leading to his death.
As a free spirit, he found inspiration in nature and wine rather than being constrained by official duties, transforming even painful experiences into poetic beauty.
Heaven born talent is inexhaustible; a million gold pieces are in vain.
One's innate talent and abilities are most important, while material wealth is only temporary.
If you do not enjoy life by raising your wine cup, how can you avoid wasting your years?
It is important to enjoy life and savor the present moment; otherwise, time will simply pass by in vain.
The green mountains remain as they were, but people have already changed.
